topic: Area of Study 2: Popular Song - Blues, Jazz, Swing and Big Band
subject: music

items.q: Is the blues always slow and sad?
items.correct: No - blues is defined by its form, harmony and blue notes; tempos and moods range from laments to up-tempo jump blues.
items.misconception: Any slow, sad song counts as a blues, and all blues are slow and sad.
items.random_wrong: Blues must always be played in a major key on a banjo.
items.why: The everyday meaning of 'feeling blue' makes students define the style by mood, but musically it is the 12-bar pattern and blues inflections that identify it.

items.q: In a swing big band, does everyone improvise at once?
items.correct: No - big band music is largely written in arrangements; improvisation is mostly given to featured soloists over the band's riffs.
items.misconception: Jazz means everyone makes it up together, so big bands improvise collectively all the time.
items.random_wrong: Big bands play entirely from memory with no arrangements at all.
items.why: Collective improvisation belongs to early New Orleans style; the swing era depended on skilled arrangers, with solo space carefully framed inside the chart.

items.correct: Deliberate vocal improvisation using nonsense syllables, treating the voice like an instrumental soloist.
items.misconception: Scat is what happens when a singer forgets the words and covers it up.
items.random_wrong: Scat is singing the same lyric in several languages.
items.why: Scat is a virtuoso art with its own technique and vocabulary; framing it as a memory lapse misses that singers like Fitzgerald improvised lines as complex as horn solos.

items.q: Scat singing is best described as what?
items.misconception: Singing the melody an octave lower
items.why: Correct answer: Improvised vocals on nonsense syllables. “Singing the melody an octave lower” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which instruments typically form the rhythm section of a swing big band?
items.correct: Piano, guitar, double bass and drums
items.why: It's Piano, guitar, double bass and drums. “Three clarinets and a tuba” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Blue notes most characteristically involve flattening which scale degrees?
items.why: Correct answer: The third, fifth and seventh. “The first and the octave” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In swing style, pairs of written quavers are usually performed how?
items.why: Correct answer: Unequally, in a long-short pattern. “Exactly as written, perfectly even” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many bars long is the classic AABA popular song chorus form?
items.why: Exact answer: 32 bars. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
